html: Introduction to Linux Operating System
1. What is an Operating System (OS)?

text: An Operating System is a system software that acts as an interface between the user and the computer hardware.
html: An Operating System is a system software that acts as an interface between the user and the computer hardware.

text: [Linux] Main functions of an Operating System
html: Main functions of an Operating System

• Controls and manages hardware (CPU, memory, disk, I/O devices)
  • Allows users to run applications
  • Manages files and folders
  • Provides security and access control

• Computer cannot work
  • User cannot interact with hardware directly

• Windows
  • Linux
  • macOS
  • Android
  • iOS

text: Linux is a free and open-source operating system based on UNIX principles.
html: Linux is a free and open-source operating system based on UNIX principles.

• Developed by Linus Torvalds (1991)
  • Open source (source code is freely available)
  • Highly secure and stable
  • Used in servers, mobiles, supercomputers, cloud, and embedded systems

• Servers (Google, Amazon, Facebook)
  • Mobile phones (Android)
  • Cloud platforms
  • Cybersecurity tools
  • Programming and development

text: [Linux] 3. Why Do We Need an Operating System?
html: 3. Why Do We Need an Operating System?

• Hardware understands only binary (0s and 1s)
  • Users understand commands and applications
  • OS converts user instructions into hardware operations

content:
User

OS

Hardware

Gives command

Translates

Executes

html: 4. Components of Linux Operating System

text: Linux operating system consists of three main components:
html: Linux operating system consists of three main components:

• Kernel
  • Shell
  • Utilities / Applications

text: The Kernel is the core (heart) of the operating system.
html: The Kernel is the core (heart) of the operating system.

text: Kernel is the central program that directly interacts with hardware and manages system resources.
html: Kernel is the central program that directly interacts with hardware and manages system resources.

• Process management (CPU scheduling)
  • Memory management
  • Device management
  • File system management
  • System call handling

text: User cannot directly access hardware  Kernel controls all hardware access
html:
User cannot directly access hardware
  Kernel controls all hardware access
6. Why Kernel is Important?

• No memory management
  • No multitasking
  • No device communication
  • System will crash

• Monolithic Kernel (Linux)
  • Microkernel
  • Hybrid Kernel

text: The Shell is a command interpreter that acts as an interface between user and kernel.
html: The Shell is a command interpreter that acts as an interface between user and kernel.

text: Shell is a program that takes user commands, interprets them, and sends them to the kernel for execution.
html: Shell is a program that takes user commands, interprets them, and sends them to the kernel for execution.
• User → Shell → Kernel → Hardware
8. Types of Shells in Linux

content:
Shell Name

Description

Bash

Most common shell

Sh

Bourne Shell

Zsh

Advanced shell

Fish

User-friendly shell

text: [Linux] 9. Why Do We Use Shell in Linux?
html: 9. Why Do We Use Shell in Linux?

• Faster than GUI
  • Powerful scripting support
  • Automates tasks
  • Required for server administration
10. Difference Between Kernel and Shell

content:
Kernel

Shell

Core of OS

Interface to OS

Manages hardware

Takes user commands

Runs in background

User interacts with it

Cannot be replaced easily

Can be changed

html: 11. Why Linux is Preferred?

• Free and open source
  • Secure and stable
  • High performance
  • Multiuser & multitasking
  • Used in real-world industry

• OS connects user and hardware
  • Linux is a free, open-source OS
  • Kernel is the heart of Linux
  • Shell is the command interface
  • Linux is powerful, secure, and widely used

text: [Linux] Linux File System & Paths (With Commands Explained Clearly)
html: Linux File System & Paths (With Commands Explained Clearly)

text: [Linux] 1. What is a Path in Linux?
html: 1. What is a Path in Linux?

text: A path is the location (address) of a file or folder inside the Linux system.
html: A path is the location (address) of a file or folder inside the Linux system.

• House address → helps you reach a house
  • Linux path → helps Linux reach a file
2. Root Directory (/) – The Starting Point

• Root directory is the top-most folder in Linux
  • Represented by /
  • All files and folders start from here

text: Linux has only one root directory
html: Linux has only one root directory
Everything like /home, /bin, /etc comes under this.

• Path that starts from root directory (/)
  • Gives complete location
  • Works from anywhere

• Start from /
  • Go to home
  • Then student
  • Then Documents
  • Open notes.txt
Command Used – cd (Change Directory)
What cd does
• Moves you from one folder to another

text: No matter where you are, Linux takes you to Documents
html: No matter where you are, Linux takes you to Documents

• Path based on current location
  • Does not start with /
  • Shorter and faster to type

text: Linux understands: “Go inside Documents which is inside my current folder”
html:
Linux understands:
“Go inside Documents which is inside my current folder”
5. pwd Command (Where Am I?)
What is pwd?

• pwd means Print Working Directory
  • Shows current location

• To avoid confusion
  • To know exactly where we are

text: Means you are inside Linux folder

html: 6. ls Command (What is Inside?)
What is ls?
• Lists files and folders in a directory

text: Like opening a bag to see what is inside
html: Like opening a bag to see what is inside
7. cd Command (Move Between Folders)
What is cd?

• Changes directory
  • Helps move inside folders
What is mkdir?
• Creates a new directory

text: A folder named LinuxNotes is created

html: 9. rmdir Command (Delete Empty Folder)
What is rmdir?
10. Special Path Symbols (Very Important)

Meaning

Example

/

/home

.

./file.txt

~

html: 11. Home Directory (~)

• Personal space for each user
  • Stores documents, downloads, files
12. Practical Real Example (Lab Style)

• pwd → Where am I?
  • ls → What is inside?
  • cd → Move folders
  • mkdir → Create folder
  • Absolute path → Full address
  • Relative path → Nearby address

text: Linux paths are addresses, and commands help us move, see, and manage those a
html: Linux paths are addresses, and commands help us move, see, and manage those a

text: LINUX FILE & DIRECTORY COMMANDS – DETAILED LECTURE NOTES
html: LINUX FILE & DIRECTORY COMMANDS – DETAILED LECTURE NOTES
1. pwd – Present Working Directory
What is pwd?

text: pwd shows where you are currently working in the Linux file system.
html: pwd shows where you are currently working in the Linux file system.

text: Linux is hierarchical (tree structure). If you don’t know your location, you may work on the wrong files.
html: Linux is hierarchical (tree structure). If you don’t know your location, you may work on the wrong files.

text: Means: You are inside the student directory.
html: Means: You are inside the student directory.

text: First you move into Documents  Then pwd confirms your new location
html:
First you move into Documents
 Then pwd confirms your new location
2. ls – List Directory Contents
What is ls?

text: Used to see files and directories inside a folder.
html: Used to see files and directories inside a folder.

text: Shows only names of files & folders
html: Shows only names of files & folders

• Permissions
  • Owner
  • Size
  • Date

text: File size shown in KB, MB instead of bytes
html: File size shown in KB, MB instead of bytes
What is cd?
What is mkdir?
5. rmdir – Remove Empty Directory
What is rmdir?
6. touch – Create File / Update Time
What is touch?

text: Creates empty files or updates timestamp.
Updates modification time if file exists
html: Updates modification time if file exists
7. cat – View File Content
What is cat?

text: Displays entire content of a file.

html: 8. less – View Large Files
What is less?

text: Used to read big files page by page.
html: Used to read big files page by page.
9. head – First Lines of File
What is head?

text: Shows top lines of a file.

html: 10. tail – Last Lines of File
What is tail?

text: Shows last lines, mainly for logs.
html: Shows last lines, mainly for logs.
11. cp – Copy Files & Directories
What is cp?
What is mv?
13. rm – Remove Files & Directories
What is find?

text: Search files based on name, type, size.
html: Search files based on name, type, size.
15. grep – Search Inside Files
What is grep?
What is which?
What is echo?

LINUX EDITORS – DETAILED LECTURE NOTES
1. What is an Editor?

text: An editor is a software tool used to create, modify, and save text files.
html: An editor is a software tool used to create, modify, and save text files.

• Configuration files
  • Program code
  • Scripts
  • Logs
    are     text-based, so editors are very important.

text: [Linux] Why Editors are Important in Linux?
html: Why Editors are Important in Linux?

• Linux is command-line driven
  • Almost everything is configured using text files
  • Editors allow:

• Editing system files
  • Writing shell scripts
  • Programming
2. Types of Editors in Linux

text: Linux editors are mainly divided into two categories:
html: Linux editors are mainly divided into two categories:

• Work inside terminal
  • Lightweight
  • Very powerful

• vi
  • vim
  • rvim
  • nano

• Mouse-based
  • Window interface

• gvim
  • gedit
  • kate
What is vi?

• Oldest and most basic Linux editor
  • Available in almost all Linux systems
  • Modal editor (works in modes)

• Lightweight
  • Keyboard-based
  • Difficult for beginners
  • Foundation for vim & gvim
What is vim?

• Syntax highlighting
  • Undo/Redo
  • Plugins support
  • Better search
What is rvim?

• Used for security
  • Restricts:

• Shell commands
  • File system access

• GUI-based vim
  • Mouse support
  • Menus & toolbar
  • Same power as vim + graphical interface

text: [Linux] Why GVIM is Best for Learning?
html: Why GVIM is Best for Learning?

text: Easy for beginners  Visual support  Same commands as vim  Used in real-world development

Easy for beginners
 Visual support
 Same commands as vim
 Used in real-world development

html: 1. What is a Wildcard Character?

text: A wildcard is a special character used in Linux shell to represent one or more characters in file or directory names.
html: A wildcard is a special character used in Linux shell to represent one or more characters in file or directory names.

text: Instead of typing full file names, we use patterns.
html: Instead of typing full file names, we use patterns.
2. Why Do We Use Wildcards?

text: Save time  Handle multiple files  Powerful file filtering  Used in ls, cp, mv, rm, find, grep
html:
Save time
 Handle multiple files
 Powerful file filtering
 Used in ls, cp, mv, rm, find, grep
3. Types of Wildcard Characters

text: Linux mainly supports these wildcard characters:
html: Linux mainly supports these wildcard characters:

content:
Wildcard

Name

*

Asterisk

?

Question mark

[ ]

Bracket expression

{ }

Brace expansion

!

Negation (inside brackets)

html: 4. Asterisk Wildcard (*)
* matches ZERO or MORE characters
⚠ Be careful with rm *
5. Question Mark (?) Wildcard
Difference Between * and ?

content:
*

?

Zero or more chars

Exactly one char

file* → file, file1

file? → file1

html: 6. Bracket Wildcard [ ]

text: [ ] matches ANY ONE character from the list or range
html: [ ] matches ANY ONE character from the list or range
6.3 Bracket with Negation [! ]
7. Brace Expansion { }

text: { } is used to generate multiple strings
html: { } is used to generate multiple strings

text: Deletes all reports not starting with numbers
html: Deletes all reports not starting with numbers
10. Important Notes for Students

text: ⚠ Wildcards are processed by shell, not command ⚠ Use ls before rm to verify ⚠ Wildcards do NOT match hidden files (.file)
html:
⚠ Wildcards are processed by shell, not command
⚠ Use ls before rm to verify
⚠ Wildcards do NOT match hidden files (.file)

text: [Linux] 1. Why Permissions Are Needed in Linux
html: 1. Why Permissions Are Needed in Linux

text: Linux is a multi-user operating system. Many users can access the same system at the same time.
html:
Linux is a multi-user operating system.
Many users can access the same system at the same time.

• Data security
  • Controlled access
  • Protection from accidental deletion
  • Proper system management
2. Types of Users in Linux

html: Every file and directory has 3 permission categories:

User (u)

Group (g)

Everyone else

• Input → Keyboard

• Thousands of commands exist

1. What is a Makefile?

text: A Makefile is a special file that contains instructions (rules) used by the make command to:
html: A Makefile is a special file that contains instructions (rules) used by the make command to:

• Compile programs
  • Build executables
  • Automate repetitive tasks
  • Save time and avoid manual compilation errors
Makefile automates the build process

text: [Linux] 2. Why Do We Need a Makefile?
html: 2. Why Do We Need a Makefile?

• Long commands
  • Easy to make mistakes
  • Recompile all files even if one file changes
  • Not scalable for big projects

text: Loop control statements are used to change the normal flow of loops.
html: Loop control statements are used to change the normal flow of loops.

• break
  • continue
1. break Statement
What is break?

• break is used to immediately stop the loop
  • Control comes outside the loop
  • Remaining iterations are skipped

text: [TCL] Script 1: Using break in a loop
html: Script 1: Using break in a loop

• i = 1 → printed
  • i = 2 → printed
  • i = 3 → condition true → break → loop stops

text: Stop checking servers when failure found
html: Stop checking servers when failure found
2. continue Statement
What is continue?

• continue skips the current iteration
  • Loop continues with next iteration
  • Loop does NOT stop

text: [TCL] Script 2: Using continue in a loop
html: Script 2: Using continue in a loop

• i = 1 → printed
  • i = 2 → printed
  • i = 3 → skipped
  • i = 4, 5 → printed
3. Difference Between break and continue

content:
Feature

break

continue

Stops loop

 Yes

 No

Skips iteration

 No

 Yes

Control

Goes outside loop

Goes to next iteration

html:
• Use break → when you want to exit loop completely
  • Use continue → when you want to skip unwanted values
  • Very useful in data validation, error handling, automation scripts

text: Decision-making statements allow a program to take decisions based on conditions.
html: Decision-making statements allow a program to take decisions based on conditions.

text: In TCL, decision making is done using:
html: In TCL, decision making is done using:

• if
  • if…else
  • if…elseif…else
  • switch
1. if Statement

• Executes a block only when condition is TRUE
  • No action when condition is FALSE
Script 1: Simple if

• Condition: age >= 18
  • True → block executed

text: Checking permission before running a task
html: Checking permission before running a task
2. if … else Statement

• Executes one block if condition is TRUE
  • Executes alternate block if FALSE
• marks < 50 → else block executed
3. if … elseif … else Statement

• Used when multiple conditions need checking
  • Executes only one matching block
• marks = 78 → second condition true
4. switch Statement

• Used for fixed value comparisons
  • Cleaner than multiple elseif
• choice = 2 → matched case 2
Comparison: if vs switch

content:
Feature

if / elseif

switch

Range checking

 Yes

 No

Exact match

 Not ideal

 Best

Readability

Medium

High

html:
• Use if → single condition
  • Use if…else → yes / no decision
  • Use if…elseif…else → multiple conditions
  • Use switch → fixed value matching
  • Decision making is core logic of automation scripts

 Writing too much logic in one procedure

• proc = reusable function
  • Arguments pass data
  • return sends result
  • Used heavily in EDA, automation, DevOps
  • Industry TCL scripts are procedure-driven
